did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used Federated Learning (a machine learning approach) to perform skin cancer classification on the HAM10000 dataset, achieving a classification accuracy of 90% or higher.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is presented at the 2023 IEEE 23rd International Conference on Bioinformatics and Bioengineering (BIBE). While it includes a literature review and analysis of FL architectures (Sections II and III), it explicitly presents original experimental verification and analysis (Section IV) using the HAM10000 dataset for skin cancer classification and the RSNA ICH Detection dataset for intracranial hemorrhage diagnosis. The abstract highlights 'two case studies' and 'detailed analysis for the results,' and the contributions include experimental demonstration, classifying it as a research paper with original results.

title: Boosting Classification Tasks with Federated Learning: Concepts, Experiments and Perspectives
journal: 2023 IEEE 23rd International Conference on Bioinformatics and Bioengineering (BIBE)
brief_summary: This study investigates the application of Federated Learning (FL) to enhance medical diagnosis efficiency and accuracy while preserving data privacy. The authors present two case studies involving skin cancer classification using the HAM10000 dataset and intracranial hemorrhage diagnosis using the RSNA ICH Detection dataset. For the skin cancer task, the model achieved a classification accuracy of 90% or higher, demonstrating the potential of FL in medical image classification. The research also analyzes current FL architectures, challenges such as privacy threats and data heterogeneity, and future research directions. The findings suggest that FL can effectively address data silos and privacy concerns in healthcare settings.

all_task_types.target: Skin cancer diagnostic categories (7 classes) and Intracranial Hemorrhage (ICH) subtypes (5 classes)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs two classification tasks: 1) Skin cancer classification using the HAM10000 dataset with 7 major groups (actinic keratoses, basal cell carcinoma, benign keratosis-like lesions, dermatofibroma, melanoma, melanocytic nevi, vascular lesions), achieving accuracy metrics (ACC, AUC, SEN, SPE). 2) Intracranial hemorrhage (ICH) diagnosis using the RSNA ICH Detection dataset with 5 subtypes (epidural, intraparenchymal, intraventricular, subarachnoid, subdural).

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A large collection of multi-source dermatoscopic images of common pigmented skin lesions, used for skin cancer classification experiments.
classes_used: actinic keratoses and intraepithelial carcinoma / Bowen’s disease (akiec)

use.training: Used for skin cancer classification experiments with a training dataset of 8512 samples.
use.testing: Used for skin cancer classification experiments with a test dataset of 1503 samples.

datasets_used.name: RSNA ICH Detection dataset
datasets_used.description: A dataset of brain CT slices provided in the original paper [58], used for intracranial hemorrhage diagnosis experiments.

use.training: Used for intracranial hemorrhage (ICH) diagnosis experiments with a training dataset of 17500 samples.
use.testing: Used for intracranial hemorrhage (ICH) diagnosis experiments with a testing dataset of 5000 samples.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Results are presented as performance metrics (Accuracy, AUC, Sensitivity, Specificity) from experimental simulations without statistical significance testing.

HAM10000.description: Skin cancer classification dataset with 7 classes. Training set: 8512 samples. Test set: 1503 samples (Melanocytic nevi: 1006, remaining 6 classes: 497).
HAM10000.model: Densenet121 with Cross-Entropy loss and Adam optimizer (FedIRM algorithm)
HAM10000.notes: Sensitivity decreased to around 10% after the 30th epoch due to unbalanced distribution of sample classes in the test set. The paper notes that the model achieved a classification accuracy of 90% or higher.

RSNA ICH Detection.description: Intracranial hemorrhage diagnosis dataset with 5 subtypes. Training set: 17500 samples. Test set: 5000 samples.
RSNA ICH Detection.model: Densenet121 with Cross-Entropy loss and Adam optimizer (FedIRM algorithm)

Original [58].description: Original paper results using small batch training with batch size 48.

Test 2.description: 2 clients (2 supervised, 8 unsupervised), batch size 8. Paper notes overfitting and continuous drop in performance.

Test 3.description: 5 clients (2 supervised, 3 unsupervised), batch size 8. Paper notes improved performance.

RSNA ICH Detection.notes: The paper compares simulation results with the original paper [58]. It states that results obtained with batch size 8 are almost identical to original results when client numbers are adjusted, though Test 2 (batch size 8, 2 clients) showed overfitting. Test 3 (5 clients) showed improved performance.

architecture_details.algorithm: FedIRM (Federated Semi-supervised Medical Image Classification via Inter-client Relation Matching)

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: FedIRM (Federated Semi-supervised Medical Image Classification via Inter-client Relation Matching)
conclusions: Federated Learning (FL) can improve the efficiency and accuracy of medical diagnosis while addressing privacy concerns by keeping data local and communicating only model parameters.
The application of FL to skin cancer classification using the HAM10000 dataset achieved a classification accuracy of 90% or higher, demonstrating its potential in medical image classification tasks.

In intracranial hemorrhage diagnosis experiments, simulation results with a batch size of 8 were found to be almost identical to original results when client numbers were adjusted, although specific configurations (e.g., 2 clients with batch size 8) showed overfitting.
Using five clients in the intracranial hemorrhage diagnosis task resulted in improved model performance, although the gain was noted to be small.

Low sensitivity in the skin cancer classification task (decreasing to around 10% after the 30th epoch) was attributed to the unbalanced distribution of sample classes in the test set, specifically the dominance of melanocytic nevi samples.

FL faces existing bottlenecks in healthcare, including challenges in improving privacy protection, addressing the negative impact of statistical heterogeneity of data on federation performance, and improving communication efficiency.
important_remarks: The skin cancer classification experiment on the HAM10000 dataset suffered from severe class imbalance in the test set, where melanocytic nevi comprised 1006 out of 1503 samples (66.9%), leading to a critically low sensitivity of 22.77% despite high accuracy (94.06%).

The intracranial hemorrhage (ICH) diagnosis experiments utilized a simulated federated learning environment rather than true multi-site data silos, which limits the assessment of real-world data heterogeneity and privacy preservation benefits.

The ICH diagnosis results were compared against an original paper [58] using a different batch size (48 vs. 8/16), raising concerns about the validity of the performance comparison due to hyperparameter differences.
The study did not report any statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values or confidence intervals) for the reported performance metrics, limiting the robustness of the comparative conclusions.

The paper acknowledges that Federated Learning faces significant bottlenecks in healthcare, including privacy threats (malicious servers/clients), statistical heterogeneity of data, and communication efficiency challenges.

In the ICH diagnosis simulations, specific configurations (e.g., Test 2 with 2 clients and batch size 8) exhibited overfitting and continuous performance drops, indicating sensitivity to client number and batch size settings.
The paper does not address external validation on independent datasets beyond the provided HAM10000 and RSNA ICH Detection datasets, restricting generalizability claims.
